How the Empress Biomarker Framework Works

Every question in the Empress diagnostic assessment is not just a symptom

check — it is a clinical signal mapped directly to measurable biomarkers,

hormonal pathways, and body systems. A woman who has never had a

blood test generates a diagnostic-grade health picture purely from how she

looks, feels, thinks, and lives.

Five dimensions per marker: (1) What we ask (0–4 severity scale) → (2)

Why it matters (clinical mechanism) → (3) System tagged → (4) What to

test (lab/wearable) → (5) First action (immediate protocol before labs).

Why This Beats Respin Health: Respin uses a brief symptom quiz to route

women to care plans. Empress maps every single symptom to its underlying

clinical biomarker, body system, confirmatory lab test, AND immediate first action

— creating a full diagnostic picture before the first consult. When labs are added,

the symptom layer and clinical layer fuse into a complete, longitudinal health

profile no other platform offers.

CATEGORY 1 — Vasomotor & Temperature Regulation

12 Markers · #01–12

The most clinically distinctive menopause symptoms. Every flash is a diagnostic event

— maps to estradiol decline rate, FSH elevation, hypothalamic thermoregulation,

cortisol reactivity, and autonomic nervous system stability.

#Symptom BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Mechanism — Why It Matters

01

Hot Flash

Frequency

How many times per day do you

experience a sudden surge of

heat?

0=Never · 1=1–2/day · 2=3–5/day ·

3=6–9/day · 4=10+/day

Each flash = FSH surge attempting to

stimulate failing ovaries. Estradiol

decline narrows the hypothalamic

thermoneutral zone to near zero.

Score ≥3 indicates clinical intervention

threshold is crossed.

02

Hot Flash SeverityWhen a hot flash hits, how intense

is it?

0=None · 1=Mild warmth · 2=Visible

flushing · 3=Drenching sweat, must

change clothes · 4=Disabling, stops all

Severity correlates with the RATE of

estrogen withdrawal — not just the

level. A fast, steep decline causes

more severe flashes than a gradual

one at the same final estradiol level.

Score 3–4 = NP referral re: HRT.

activity

03

Night Sweat

Frequency

How many nights per week do you

wake drenched in sweat?

0=Never · 1=1–2 nights/week · 2=3–4

nights · 3=5–6 nights · 4=Every single

night

Nocturnal vasomotor events destroy

deep sleep and REM architecture

within 48 hours. Every disrupted night

04

Hot Flash DurationHow long does a typical hot flash

last from start to finish?

0=None · 1=Under 1 min · 2=1–3 mins ·

3=3–5 mins · 4=Over 5 minutes

Duration reflects depth of

hypothalamic dysregulation. Flashes

over 3 minutes indicate sustained

adrenergic activation — the body is

failing to quickly self-correct the

temperature response. Indicates

significant FSH elevation.

05

Palpitations

During Flashes

Do you feel your heart racing or

pounding during or immediately

after a hot flash?

0=Never · 1=Occasionally · 2=With

most flashes · 3=Intense racing ·

4=Every flash + prolonged palpitation

Each flash triggers an adrenergic

surge — adrenaline spike causes

tachycardia, heart rate can spike 10–

30 bpm per flash. Score ≥3 requires

cardiac and thyroid rule-out before

attributing to menopause alone.

06

Chills After FlashAfter a hot flash ends, do you

immediately shiver or feel

intensely cold?

0=Never · 4=Every flash followed by

chills

Overcorrection of the hypothalamic

thermostat. The body over-activates

heat dissipation mechanisms then

overcorrects to cold. Indicates high

hypothalamic sensitivity to even tiny

estrogen fluctuations.

07

Cold Hands & FeetDo your hands and feet feel

chronically cold even when the

rest of your body is comfortable or

flushing?

0=No change from before ·

4=Constantly cold extremities

regardless of temperature

Peripheral vasoconstriction signals

thyroid insufficiency (Free T3 drives

peripheral blood flow), low ferritin

(poor oxygen delivery to extremities),

or progesterone deficiency

(progesterone is a natural

vasodilator). Combination of cold

extremities + fatigue + hair thinning =

hypothyroid cluster — order TSH

urgently.

08

Heat IntoleranceHave you become significantly

more sensitive to heat —

overheating in environments

others find comfortable?

0=No change · 4=Cannot tolerate

normal ambient temperatures

The thermoneutral zone — the

temperature range the body is

comfortable in — narrows from ~4°C

to near 0°C with estrogen loss. Any

warmth triggers the flash cascade.

Can also indicate thyroid

hyperfunctioning (hyperthyroid

produces separate heat intolerance —

must rule out).

09

Alcohol as Flash

Trigger

Does alcohol — even a single

glass — reliably trigger a hot flash

within 1–2 hours?

Alcohol disrupts hepatic estrogen

metabolism — impairs the liver's

clearance of estrogen metabolites,

causing erratic swings that trigger the

CATEGORY 2 — Sleep Architecture & Cortisol Rhythm

11 Markers · #13–23

Sleep disruption is the single greatest amplifier of every other menopause symptom.

Poor sleep makes hot flashes worse, brain fog worse, weight gain worse, mood worse.

Fix sleep first — everything else improves. Maps primarily to progesterone, cortisol

diurnal rhythm, and melatonin.

0=Don't drink / No effect ·

1=Occasionally · 2=Usually · 3=Almost

always · 4=Even small amounts always

trigger

hypothalamic response. Also directly

depletes progesterone and destroys

REM sleep architecture. GGT is the

liver estrogen clearance marker

elevated by regular alcohol use.

10

Stress-Triggered

Flashes

Do emotional stress or anxiety

episodes reliably precede or

intensify hot flashes?

0=No connection · 4=Every stressful

moment triggers a flash

Cortisol and norepinephrine both

independently narrow the

low AND cortisol is high, the triggers

become multiplicative — any

emotional event becomes a flash

trigger. This pattern predicts HPA axis

dysregulation and warrants 4-point

diurnal cortisol testing.

11

Caffeine as Flash

Trigger

Does coffee, tea, or caffeinated

beverages reliably worsen or

trigger hot flashes?

0=No effect · 4=Any caffeine

immediately triggers a flash

Caffeine activates the sympathetic

nervous system — same pathway as

estrogen-loss-induced

thermoregulatory instability. In

estrogen-depleted women, caffeine

receptor sensitivity significantly

increases. The cortisol spike from

caffeine narrows the thermoneutral

zone further.

12

Facial Flushing

(Non-Flash)

Do you experience facial redness,

rosacea-like flushing, or visible

skin blotchiness separate from hot

flashes?

0=Never · 4=Daily persistent facial

redness visible to others

Estrogen loss reduces facial blood

vessel wall integrity. Histamine

intolerance — which worsens post-

menopause — also causes flushing.

Gut dysbiosis producing histamine-

secreting bacteria (Klebsiella,

Morganella) is an underrecognised

driver. Can indicate mast cell

activation disorder emerging in

perimenopause.

#Symptom BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Mechanism — Why It Matters

13

Sleep OnsetHow long does it take to fall

Sleep onset latency >30 minutes is

clinically significant. Primary drivers:

Latencyasleep after getting into bed?

0=Under 15 mins · 1=15–30 mins ·

2=30–60 mins · 3=1–2 hours · 4=Over

2 hours or cannot sleep

progesterone deficiency (removes

GABA-A sedating action — the brain's

natural benzodiazepine mechanism),

elevated evening cortisol, and low

serotonin producing racing thoughts.

Score ≥2 = progesterone almost

certainly deficient.

14

Night Wakings —

Frequency

How many times do you wake

after initially falling asleep?

0=None · 1=Once · 2=2–3 times · 3=4–

5 times · 4=So frequently I cannot count

Sleep fragmentation destroys slow-

wave deep sleep (physically

restorative) and REM

(cognitively/emotionally restorative).

Every waking resets the sleep stage

ladder from scratch. Women with 3+

wakings per night generate less than

30 minutes of deep sleep — the

minimum threshold for physical repair.

15

2–4am Waking

Pattern

Do you specifically wake between

2am and 4am and find it nearly

impossible to return to sleep?

0=Never · 1=Occasionally · 2=Weekly ·

3=Most nights · 4=Every single night

This precise window is the cortisol

inversion signature. Normal cortisol

bottoms at midnight and peaks at

8am. In HPA-dysregulated women,

cortisol starts rising at 2am — a stress

hormone alarm in the middle of the

night. Blood glucose also drops in this

window in insulin-resistant women,

triggering a cortisol release as

compensation.

16

Wired-But-Tired

Paradox

Do you feel physically exhausted

during the day yet become

mentally alert, wired, or anxious at

bedtime?

0=Never · 4=Every night without

exception

Classic cortisol circadian inversion.

Evening cortisol — which should be

near-zero — is elevated. The brain

interprets this as "daytime" and

suppresses melatonin. The body is

simultaneously exhausted (adrenal

fatigue during day) and unable to

sleep (cortisol spike at night). Often

paired with evening anxiety and the

doom-feeling described in Cat 4.

17

Sleep Satisfaction

on Waking

How rested do you feel physically

and mentally when you wake up?

content:
0=Fully rested · 1=Mostly rested ·

2=Somewhat tired · 3=Very tired ·

4=Exhausted — as if I never slept

Unrestorative sleep despite adequate

hours = deep sleep (N3) suppression.

Deep sleep is where growth hormone

is released, immune repair occurs,

and metabolic waste is cleared from

the brain via the glymphatic system.

Chronic suppression = accelerated

aging, poor immunity, worsening brain

fog, increased dementia risk.

18

Morning Body LagHow long after waking does your

body feel physically functional —

not just tired, but stiff, slow,

unreachable?

0=Within 15 mins · 1=15–30 mins ·

2=30–60 mins · 3=1–2 hours · 4=Never

really functional until afternoon

Prolonged morning body lag signals

cortisol awakening response (CAR)

blunting — the normal morning

cortisol spike that wakes the body is

absent or insufficient. Low Free T3 is

a major independent contributor:

every cell in the body needs T3 to

produce energy, and if T3 is low,

morning startup is slow across every

system simultaneously.

19

Dream Intensity &

Nightmares

Have you noticed significantly

more vivid, emotionally intense, or

disturbing dreams than before the

transition?

0=No change · 2=More vivid · 3=Often

disturbing · 4=Nightly nightmares that

wake you

REM rebound from chronic REM

suppression — when the brain finally

reaches REM (often only in the

second half of the night), it

overexpresses it with intense,

threatening content driven by elevated

norepinephrine. Vivid nightmares

specifically indicate high nocturnal

cortisol or norepinephrine. Tracks with

anxiety and HPA axis dysregulation.

20

Restless Legs at

Night

Do you experience uncomfortable

sensations in your legs at night —

crawling, tingling, aching, or an

irresistible urge to move?

0=Never · 4=Every night, severely

disrupts sleep

Restless leg syndrome (RLS)

prevalence increases significantly in

menopause. Primary driver: low

ferritin — iron is required for

dopamine synthesis in the substantia

nigra, and dopamine deficiency

directly causes the RLS sensation.

Ferritin target for RLS resolution is

>75 ng/mL — most women test

"normal" at 12–15 but remain

symptomatic until ferritin is higher.

21

Sleep

Environment

Sensitivity

Have you become newly sensitive

to noise, light, or temperature

variations that previously had no

effect on your sleep?

0=No change · 4=Any minor disruption

wakes me completely

Heightened CNS arousal threshold —

the brain's sleep protective gating has

weakened. Estrogen and

progesterone normally dampen the

arousal system; their loss leaves the

nervous system hypervigilant, unable

to filter sensory input during sleep.

This symptom improves rapidly with

progesterone support in most women.

22

Daytime

Sleepiness

Do you feel irresistibly sleepy

during the day — particularly mid-

morning or mid-afternoon — even

after a full night in bed?

0=Rarely · 4=Constant debilitating

sleepiness

Daytime hypersomnolence despite

adequate time in bed = sleep quality

failure, not quantity failure. The body

is completing hours but not sleep

stages. Thyroid insufficiency is a

major independent contributor —

every organ starved of T3 function

signals fatigue. These two causes

(sleep quality + thyroid) must be

assessed simultaneously.

23

Nap DependencyDo you feel you NEED to nap to

function — and does missing a

nap significantly impair your

afternoon capacity?

0=Never nap / Don't need to · 4=Daily

content:
nap is non-negotiable, skip it and I

cannot function

New-onset nap dependency in midlife

indicates: (1) primary sleep quality

failure (most likely), (2) thyroid

insufficiency (body cannot sustain

energy through day), or (3) adrenal

burnout (cortisol production dropped

below what is needed for sustained

alertness). All three can coexist and

all three require lab investigation.

CATEGORY 3 — Cognitive Function & Brain Health

12 Markers · #24–35

Brain fog is the most distressing and most dismissed menopause symptom. It is a

neurological event — not a personality flaw. Maps to BDNF, acetylcholine, cerebral

glucose metabolism, and estrogen's direct neuroprotective role. Empress is the only

platform that treats this as a clinical signal, not a complaint.

#Symptom BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Mechanism — Why It Matters

24

Word-Finding

Difficulty

How often do you struggle to

retrieve the right word mid-

sentence — you know it exists but

cannot access it?

0=Never · 1=Rarely · 2=Several

times/day · 3=Constantly, embarrassing

· 4=Impacts every conversation

Estrogen directly upregulates choline

acetyltransferase — the enzyme

synthesising acetylcholine, the

primary verbal retrieval

neurotransmitter. As estradiol drops,

acetylcholine synthesis falls

specifically in left-hemisphere verbal

memory circuits. This is why word-

finding is the FIRST cognitive

symptom to appear in perimenopause

— before memory or processing

speed decline.

25

Short-Term

Memory Lapses

How often do you forget things

you just did — walked into a room

forgetting why, missed

appointments you scheduled,

repeated a question you just

asked?

0=Occasional/normal · 1=Slightly more

· 2=Noticeably frequent · 3=Multiple

times daily · 4=Severe and frightening

Hippocampal volume reduction is

measurable on MRI in

perimenopausal women — the

hippocampus has the highest density

of estrogen receptors in the brain.

Sleep deprivation compounds this:

short-term memories require deep

sleep to consolidate to long-term

storage. Without deep sleep,

memories formed during the day are

literally lost overnight. Score ≥3 =

ApoE genotyping recommended.

26

Concentration

Span

How long can you hold focused

attention on a single task before

your mind wanders

uncontrollably?

0=45+ mins as before · 1=30–45 mins ·

2=15–30 mins · 3=5–15 mins · 4=Under

5 mins

Sustained attention relies on

noradrenergic and dopaminergic

function in the prefrontal cortex —

both regulated by estrogen. Attention

span reduction is one of the earliest

measurable cognitive effects and is

highly responsive to estrogen

restoration. When cortisol is also high,

it further suppresses prefrontal

function (high cortisol literally shrinks

prefrontal dendrites over time).

27

Mental Processing

Speed

Does your thinking feel

measurably slower — a lag before

your brain responds — compared

to 2–3 years ago?

Cerebral glucose metabolism declines

with estrogen loss — the brain

becomes less efficient at using

glucose as fuel. This is the same

0=No change · 4=Significantly slower,

affects work and conversations daily

mechanism underlying Alzheimer's

risk in women: years before any

plaques form, glucose

hypometabolism is measurable on

PET scan. MCT oil can bypass this

impairment by providing ketones as

an alternative brain fuel source.

28

Spatial Memory

Loss

Do you frequently misplace

everyday objects — keys, phone,

glasses — in places that would

0=No more than before · 4=Multiple

times per day, completely unlike the

Spatial navigation neurons in the

hippocampus and parietal cortex are

among the most estrogen-sensitive in

the brain. New spatial memory loss in

perimenopause is a strong

hippocampal vulnerability flag.

Combined with memory lapses and

processing speed decline, it warrants

BDNF testing and ApoE genotyping

— this is Empress's clinical early-

warning triplet for cognitive risk.

29

Multi-Tasking

Capacity

Has your ability to manage

multiple simultaneous tasks

declined significantly from what

you previously handled with ease?

0=No change · 4=Can barely manage

one task; juggling is impossible

Working memory capacity — the

cognitive workspace holding multiple

threads simultaneously — is

dopamine-dependent in the prefrontal

cortex. Estrogen directly modulates

D1 dopamine receptor density in this

region. Its loss literally reduces the

brain's parallel-processing capacity.

This is not burnout — it is a

measurable neurochemical change.

30

Decision FatigueDo even minor decisions — what

to eat, what to wear, how to reply

to an email — feel

disproportionately exhausting or

anxiety-producing?

0=Not at all · 4=Simple decisions feel

overwhelming and paralyzing

Decision fatigue is the convergence of

prefrontal depletion (low dopamine),

elevated baseline anxiety (low

progesterone), and blood glucose

dysregulation (poor glucose supply to

executive cortex). Decision quality

deteriorates throughout the day as

glucose is depleted — worsened by

insulin resistance. Protein breakfast is

the most immediate intervention.

31

Reading Re-

Reading

Do you need to re-read the same

paragraph multiple times before its

meaning registers — even content

you'd normally find easy?

0=Never · 4=Almost every time I try to

read anything complex

Working memory loop disruption:

information enters but is displaced

before it can be processed because

the attentional buffer is too small or

too unstable. Sleep deprivation

specifically devastates the working

memory loop — reading

comprehension degrades measurably

after 2+ weeks of fragmented sleep.

REM sleep is specifically required for

semantic encoding.

32

Mental Clarity on

Waking

How quickly does your mind feel

genuinely clear and sharp after

waking?

The cortisol awakening response

(CAR) should produce a 50–100%

cortisol spike within 30 minutes of

waking — the brain's chemical

0=Within 30 mins · 1=30–60 mins ·

2=1–2 hours · 3=Not until afternoon ·

4=Never feels fully clear all day

ignition. When CAR is blunted (from

HPA exhaustion), cognitive startup is

slow. Free T3 adds an independent

layer: it is required for

neurotransmitter synthesis and every

cellular energy reaction including in

neurons.

33

Number &

Calculation

Difficulty

Have you noticed new difficulty

with mental arithmetic,

remembering numbers, or

managing financial tasks you

previously handled with ease?

0=No change · 4=Significant new

difficulty that concerns me

The parietal cortex — responsible for

numerical processing and spatial

calculation — is highly estrogen-

sensitive. New dyscalculia-like

symptoms in menopause are a

genuine neurological marker and

should not be dismissed. This is an

early warning biomarker for estrogen-

related cognitive vulnerability. Score

≥2 = ApoE genotyping and BDNF

measurement recommended.

34

Confidence

Decline

Has your confidence in your ability

to perform professionally declined

in the last 12 months — not your

actual performance but your self-

trust?

0=No change · 4=Fundamentally

undermined, considering stepping back

Cognitive symptoms + anxiety + poor

sleep create a vicious self-

assessment spiral: fog causes errors

→ errors cause anxiety → anxiety

worsens fog → she concludes she is

"no longer capable." This is primarily

hormonal. Testosterone loss

specifically reduces drive and

professional assertiveness — free

testosterone panel is indicated. The

woman's actual capability has not

declined — her hormonal support for

executing it has.

35

Mental EnduranceHow long can you sustain

demanding intellectual work —

writing, analysis, complex

problem-solving — before hitting a

cognitive wall?

0=Full day as before · 1=4–6 hrs · 2=2–

4 hrs · 3=Under 2 hrs · 4=Under 1 hour

before complete shutdown

Cognitive stamina parallels

mitochondrial efficiency in neurons.

As estrogen declines, neuronal

mitochondria become less efficient at

ATP production — cognitive work

becomes energetically more

expensive. Insulin resistance

compounds this by reducing glucose

availability to the brain. Women with

score ≥3 have a significant metabolic-

brain intersection problem requiring

metabolic investigation.

CATEGORY 4 — Mood, Anxiety & Emotional Health

13 Markers · #36–48

The most under-treated and most stigmatised dimension. Women are told they are

anxious or depressed — when they are progesterone-deficient and estrogen-

fluctuating. These are not psychological failings. They are neurochemical events with

measurable hormonal fingerprints and highly effective treatments.

#Symptom BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Mechanism — Why It Matters

36

Baseline Anxiety

Level

Describe the resting, background

level of anxiety you carry through

your day — worry or unease

without a specific trigger.

0=None · 1=Mild background hum ·

2=Noticeable but manageable ·

3=Persistent and disruptive · 4=Severe

— near-constant dread or agitation

Progesterone's neurosteroid

metabolite allopregnanolone is the

most potent natural GABA-A receptor

agonist the body produces — the

body's own benzodiazepine. As

progesterone falls in perimenopause,

this calming mechanism is removed.

The nervous system becomes

chronically under-inhibited. Estrogen

also directly upregulates serotonin

receptor density. Score ≥3 = NP

progesterone assessment urgently.

37

Feeling of Dread

or Doom

Do you experience unexplained

feelings of dread, foreboding, or

doom — particularly on waking or

at dusk — without a clear external

cause?

0=Never · 4=Daily, specifically upon

waking or at dusk

This is the single most specific clinical

marker of progesterone deficiency.

The morning dread pattern (upon

waking before the day begins) and the

evening anxiety pattern (as daylight

fades) are signature allopregnanolone

withdrawal phenomena. Women with

this symptom at score ≥2 have near-

clinical certainty of significant

progesterone deficiency. No other

hormone or neurotransmitter

produces this precise experiential

signature.

38

Irritability &

Frustration

Threshold

How quickly do you become

irritated by minor annoyances that

previously would not have affected

you?

0=No change · 4=Hair-trigger irritability

constantly, not the person I recognise

Serotonin sets the irritability threshold

content:
— it is the emotional buffer between a

stimulus and a reaction. Estrogen

upregulates serotonin receptor

binding and reuptake transporter

expression. As estradiol fluctuates

and falls, the serotonin buffer

disappears. Minor stimuli produce

disproportionate reactions. This is

biochemical — not a character failure.

It normalises with hormonal support.

39

Rage EpisodesHave you experienced sudden,

explosive anger — completely

disproportionate to the situation

Perimenopause rage is a distinct and

underrecognised clinical

phenomenon. The mechanism: high-

and profoundly unlike your pre-

menopause personality?

0=Never · 4=Weekly explosive

episodes that frighten me or others

amplitude estrogen surges (in early

perimenopause, levels can spike very

high before falling) combined with

simultaneous progesterone deficiency.

The estrogen surge drives arousal

and activation; without progesterone's

GABA inhibition, there is no emotional

brake. This is a neurochemical event

— not a character flaw.

40

Emotional

Volatility

Do your emotions swing

dramatically within a single day —

from stable to tearful to anxious to

irritable without proportionate

external triggers?

0=Emotionally stable · 4=Severe

unpredictable swings throughout every

day

Erratic estradiol fluctuation directly

destabilises limbic system emotional

regulation circuits. Emotional volatility

TRACKS the estradiol curve — on

days when estradiol spikes, mood

may be elevated; on crash days,

depression and tearfulness follow.

The unpredictability is itself the clinical

marker of erratic perimenopause

estrogen fluctuation, not sustained low

estrogen.

41

Depressive

Episodes

Do you experience periods of low

mood, sadness, or loss of interest

that are new or significantly worse

during this transition — not

attributable to specific life

circumstances?

0=None · 1=Mild occasional ·

2=Moderate · 3=Significant, most days ·

4=Severe, impairs all functioning

Perimenopause is the highest-risk

period for a new depressive episode

in a woman's life — higher risk than

postpartum. Mechanism: estrogen

withdrawal depresses serotonin AND

dopamine AND norepinephrine

simultaneously — a neurochemical

trifecta of mood suppression. Score

≥3 requires PHQ-9 administration and

NP clinical assessment, not just

reassurance.

42

Emotional

Numbness

Do you feel emotionally flat,

disconnected, or numb — unable

to feel joy, excitement, or

connection to the degree you once

did?

0=Fully emotionally present ·

4=Persistent flatness, disconnected

from things that used to matter

Anhedonia and emotional blunting are

dopaminergic symptoms — the

reward system is underactivated.

Estrogen directly modulates dopamine

synthesis in the mesolimbic reward

circuit. Loss of estrogen = reduction in

baseline dopamine tone = persistent

flat affect and inability to experience

anticipatory pleasure. Often

misdiagnosed as depression when it

is primarily hormonal dopamine

insufficiency.

43

Panic AttacksHave you experienced sudden

episodes of intense fear, racing

heart, breathlessness, or feeling of

unreality without a clear physical

trigger?

0=Never · 4=Weekly or more frequently

Panic attacks in perimenopause are

driven by adrenergic hypersensitivity

— estrogen normally modulates

epinephrine receptor sensitivity and its

loss makes the sympathetic nervous

system hyperreactive. TSH must be

tested urgently — thyroid disease

Afternoon Low /

Flat Spell

Do you experience a pronounced

mid-to-late afternoon emotional

low — flatness, sadness, or quiet

despair — that lifts somewhat by

0=No pattern · 4=Every afternoon 2–

5pm feels emotionally dark

The 2–5pm cortisol trough coincides

with the daily low of multiple

neurotransmitter systems. In

estrogen-deficient women, this normal

trough becomes a pronounced mood

valley because estrogen normally

sustains serotonin activity to smooth

circadian mood variation. This

symptom pattern is highly specific to

estrogen insufficiency and highly

responsive to estrogen stabilisation.

45

Loss of Motivation

/ Drive

Have you lost the motivation,

ambition, or drive that previously

defined you — not from

depression but from a flat inability

to care or initiate?

0=No change · 4=Complete

motivational flatness, cannot initiate

even pleasurable activities

This is testosterone's fingerprint. Free

testosterone drives dopaminergic

reward anticipation, goal-directed

behaviour, and competitive motivation

in women. Women lose 50% of

testosterone by age 40 — this

symptom often PRECEDES estrogen

symptoms by years. Combined with

estrogen decline in perimenopause,

motivational flatness can be profound

and is frequently misattributed to

burnout or personality change.

46

Crying Easily /

Tearfulness

Do you cry far more easily than

before — at things that previously

wouldn't have moved you, or cry

without knowing why?

0=No change · 4=Crying multiple times

daily, often unprovoked

Tearfulness without proportionate

external cause is a direct serotonin

and estrogen withdrawal symptom.

The emotional threshold for crying is

regulated by serotonin tone — as

estrogen falls and destabilises

serotonin, the threshold lowers

dramatically. Unprovoked crying (no

emotional trigger) is specifically a

strong marker of progesterone

deficiency and allopregnanolone

withdrawal.

47

Social WithdrawalHave you been withdrawing from

social activities, friendships, or

events you previously enjoyed —

not from circumstance but from

internal discomfort?

0=No change · 4=Significant self-

imposed isolation, avoiding almost all

social contact

Social withdrawal is the compound

product of anxiety (fear of hot flash or

panic in public), low self-esteem

(appearance and cognitive confidence

decline), emotional numbness

(nothing feels worth the effort), and

fatigue (social energy is unavailable).

Each has a distinct hormonal driver —

the social withdrawal is downstream

of all of them simultaneously. Social

isolation also directly worsens

inflammatory markers (IL-6, CRP)

creating a feedback loop.

48

Identity

Disconnection

Do you feel the person you are

now — emotionally, cognitively,

and physically — is fundamentally

unrecognisable from the person

you were 3–5 years ago?

Identity disconnection is the

composite expression of all other

mood, cognitive, and physical

symptoms accumulating to a

threshold where the woman's sense

of self has been disrupted. It is not

psychiatric — it is the subjective

Unexplained

Weight Gain

Have you gained weight in the

past 1–3 years unexplained by

diet or activity changes —

particularly around the abdomen?

0=No change · 1=1–5 lbs · 2=5–10 lbs ·

3=10–20 lbs · 4=Over 20 lbs despite no

Estrogen regulates adipose tissue

distribution — in its presence, fat is

stored peripherally (hips/thighs). In its

absence, fat redistributes viscerally

(abdomen). Simultaneously: estrogen

loss reduces insulin sensitivity, cortisol

increases visceral fat storage, and

resting metabolic rate decreases via

muscle loss. Three independent

mechanisms drive weight gain

simultaneously. Calorie restriction

alone fails because it doesn't address

any of them.

50

Abdominal

Bloating

Do you experience abdominal

distension or bloating —

particularly after meals, in the

afternoon, or cyclically?

0=No change · 4=Daily severe bloating,

distension visible, impacts clothing

Three distinct drivers: (1) declining

estrogen alters gut motility and

reduces gastric acid production; (2)

progesterone loss removes its smooth

muscle relaxant effect on the gut,

causing delayed motility; (3)

estrobolome dysbiosis — the gut

bacteria responsible for estrogen

recirculation become imbalanced,

producing excess gas-forming

bacteria. Treating only with antacids

misses all three root causes.

51

Sugar & Carb

Cravings

Have sugar and carbohydrate

cravings significantly increased —

Estrogen directly sensitises insulin

receptors. As estradiol declines,

insulin sensitivity falls — cells become

particularly in the afternoon,

evening, or around sleep

0=No change · 4=Intense, hard-to-resist

carb cravings daily

less responsive to glucose, the brain's

glucose demand triggers cravings to

compensate. Simultaneously, low

serotonin drives carbohydrate

cravings because carbs temporarily

elevate serotonin — the craving is the

brain's self-medication attempt.

Berberine addresses both

mechanisms.

52

Loss of Muscle

Tone

Have you noticed loss of muscle

tone or firmness — particularly

arms, thighs, and abdomen —

even with the same or more

exercise as before?

0=No change · 4=Significant visible

muscle loss throughout the body

Estrogen supports myosin heavy

chain synthesis — the primary

structural protein of muscle fibres.

Testosterone drives satellite cell

activation for muscle repair. Both fall

in perimenopause, creating

sarcopenia that begins up to a decade

before menopause. After 40, women

lose approximately 1–2% of muscle

mass per year without targeted

resistance training. Creatine is the

most evidence-backed supplement for

reversing this.

53

Inability to Lose

Weight

Have strategies that previously

worked for weight loss become

ineffective or counterproductive

now?

0=Normal response · 4=Complete

resistance — doing everything right and

nothing moves

Energy Crashes

After Eating

Do you experience significant

energy crashes or brain fog within

1–2 hours of eating — particularly

after carbohydrate-containing

meals?

0=No effect · 4=Every carb meal

causes a crash taking hours to recover

from

Post-meal energy crashes are the

clinical signature of insulin

dysregulation — blood glucose spikes

rapidly then falls sharply (reactive

hypoglycaemia) causing a dopamine

and norepinephrine drop that

manifests as drowsiness, fog, and

irritability. In menopause, estrogen

loss reduces insulin receptor

sensitivity, making these swings more

pronounced. Continuous glucose

monitoring reveals this pattern

objectively.

55

Fluid Retention /

Puffiness

Do you experience puffiness or

fluid retention — face, hands,

ankles, abdomen — that is new or

significantly worse?

0=No change · 4=Daily puffiness,

visible and uncomfortable

Estrogen has direct effects on

aldosterone (fluid-regulating hormone)

and vascular permeability. High-

amplitude estrogen surges in early

perimenopause can cause significant

water retention. Progesterone is a

natural diuretic — its loss compounds

retention. Score ≥2 with cyclical

pattern = characteristic early

perimenopause fluid fluctuation driven

by erratic estrogen.

56

Increased Hunger

/ Never Satisfied

Do you feel hungry more

frequently or do meals fail to

satisfy you the way they used to

— a persistent sense of

incompleteness after eating?

0=Normal appetite · 4=Constant hunger

regardless of what or how much I eat

Leptin resistance develops in

menopause — leptin is the satiety

hormone, and estrogen normally

sensitises leptin receptors in the

hypothalamus. With estrogen loss, the

brain never receives the "satisfied"

signal. Ghrelin (hunger hormone)

levels also rise. This is a physiological

drive to eat more — not a lack of

willpower. High protein intake is the

only macronutrient that can partially

override leptin resistance.

57

Waist

Circumference

Change

Has your waist circumference

increased — comparing to 2–3

years ago — even if overall weight

has not changed dramatically?

0=No change · 1=Under 1 inch · 2=1–2

inches · 3=2–4 inches · 4=Over 4

inches

Waist circumference is the single

most clinically predictive measure of

metabolic syndrome and

cardiovascular risk in women over 40

— more predictive than BMI. A waist

over 35 inches (88cm) defines

abdominal obesity. The visceral fat

deposited in the menopausal

abdomen is metabolically active — it

secretes inflammatory cytokines (IL-6,

TNF-alpha) directly worsening insulin

resistance and cardiovascular risk.

58

Exercise

Intolerance

Has your exercise tolerance

significantly declined — becoming

exhausted or requiring much

longer recovery from workouts that

previously felt manageable?

0=No change · 4=Exercise leaves me

bedridden for 1–2 days

Mitochondrial efficiency declines with

estrogen loss — cells require more

oxygen and produce more oxidative

stress per unit of work. Iron deficiency

(common, often masked post-

perimenopause by cycle irregularity)

The afternoon cortisol trough in

dramatically deeper than normal.

Without estrogen's buffering effect on

the HPA axis, the adrenal glands

produce insufficient cortisol in the

recovery phase after the morning

peak. Caffeine cannot fix this — it

requires adrenal support and blood

sugar management. Coffee at this

time worsens evening cortisol

The most visible signs of the transition. Women lose 30% of skin collagen in the first 5

years post-menopause. Hair thinning and nail changes are direct hormonal indicators

declines in menopause through three

mass loss (muscle is the primary

metabolic tissue); (2) thyroid function

Skin DrynessHas your skin become significantly

drier, tighter, or flakier — requiring

far more moisturisation than

before?

0=No change · 4=Severe dryness,

cracking, constant discomfort

Estrogen receptors are throughout the

dermis and epidermis. Estrogen

regulates sebum production (natural

skin oil), hyaluronic acid synthesis

(water retention), and aquaporin

expression (water transport channels).

As estrogen declines, all three reduce

simultaneously — the dryness cannot

be resolved by topical moisturiser

alone. Internal intervention is required.

62

Collagen Loss /

Skin Thinning

Has your skin become noticeably

thinner, more papery, or more

prone to bruising? Do you see

visible loss of firmness or facial

volume?

0=No change · 4=Dramatic thinning,

significant facial hollowing

Women lose approximately 30% of

skin collagen in the first 5 years after

menopause — roughly 2% per year.

Estrogen stimulates fibroblast activity

(collagen-producing cells). Without

estrogen, fibroblasts slow production

and collagen fibres break down faster

than replaced. Measurable,

predictable, and partially reversible

with targeted internal and topical

intervention.

63

Increase

Has your skin become more

reactive or irritated by products,

fabrics, sun, or temperature that

previously caused no reaction?

0=No change · 4=Skin reacts to almost

Estrogen maintains skin barrier

integrity via tight junction proteins and

ceramide regulation. Its loss leaves

the barrier porous — irritants

penetrate more easily, immune cells in

Adult Acne /

Jawline Breakouts

Have you developed new adult

acne or increased breakouts —

particularly on the chin, jaw, or

lower face — that are new in this

transition?

0=No change · 4=Significant ongoing

breakouts, deeply unlike previous skin

behaviour

Hormonal acne in perimenopause is

driven by relative androgen excess —

estrogen and progesterone fall faster

than testosterone, leaving

testosterone relatively unopposed.

Testosterone — now unbalanced —

stimulates sebaceous glands. Jawline

and chin distribution is the classic

androgen-distribution pattern. This is

the OPPOSITE of teenage acne

hormonally — treating with the same

products is ineffective.

65

Facial Hair GrowthHave you developed new or

increased hair growth on the chin,

upper lip, jaw, or cheeks?

0=None · 1=A few stray hairs ·

2=Noticeable, requires regular removal

· 3=Significant, socially distressing ·

4=Heavy growth, major confidence

impact

Same relative androgen excess

mechanism as acne: testosterone

unopposed by falling

estrogen/progesterone activates

androgen-sensitive facial follicles.

Combined with abdominal weight

gain, this warrants assessment for

PCOS residual pattern or adrenal

androgen excess. DHEA-S elevation

indicates adrenal contribution —

common in perimenopausal women

who have been under chronic stress.

66

Scalp Hair

Thinning

Have you noticed decreased hair

density, a widening part, increased

daily shedding, or visible scalp

through hair that was previously

thick?

0=No change · 4=Significant thinning

and shedding, visible scalp in multiple

areas

Female pattern hair loss accelerates

in menopause: (1) relative androgen

excess affects androgen-sensitive

scalp follicles at temples and crown;

(2) estrogen normally extends the

growth phase of follicles — its loss

shortens it. Ferritin deficiency is a

major, frequently MISSED

independent driver — most women

test "normal" at 12–15 ng/mL but hair

loss resolves only when ferritin

exceeds 70–80 ng/mL.

67

Outer Eyebrow

Thinning

Have your eyebrows become

noticeably thinner — particularly

the outer third — or your

eyelashes shorter and more

sparse?

0=No change · 4=Outer third of

eyebrows nearly absent

Outer-third eyebrow thinning is a

classic clinical sign of hypothyroidism

— the thyroid gland controls the

anagen (growth) phase of these

specific follicles. This is a textbook

physical examination finding in

medical training. The combination of

outer eyebrow loss + fatigue + cold

hands + hair thinning + weight gain =

hypothyroid cluster requiring

URGENT TSH testing. Score ≥2 =

order thyroid panel today.

68

Nail Brittleness &Have your nails become more

Nail plate integrity requires: keratin

Ridgingbrittle, peeling, breaking easily, or

developed vertical ridges not

previously present?

0=No change · 4=Nails constantly

breaking, severe ridging

(needs adequate protein and iron),

zinc (keratinocyte function), biotin (nail

matrix cell division), and estrogen-

maintained connective tissue

hydration. Vertical ridges =

micronutrient deficiency; brittleness =

protein or iron insufficiency; white

spots = zinc deficiency. The nail is a

visible micronutrient readout that

requires no lab to interpret.

Slow Wound

Healing

Have you noticed that cuts,

scratches, or skin injuries take

significantly longer to heal than

before?

0=No change · 4=Wounds taking 2–3x

as long to heal

Estrogen accelerates wound healing

through three mechanisms: direct

fibroblast stimulation, anti-

inflammatory macrophage regulation,

and VEGF upregulation for new blood

vessel formation. Slow wound healing

is also an early indicator of impaired

glucose metabolism —

hyperglycaemia impairs neutrophil

function and collagen synthesis.

Score ≥2 = test HbA1c alongside

estrogen assessment.

70

Hyperpigmentation

/ Melasma

Have you developed new dark

patches, melasma-style

discolouration, or uneven skin

tone on the face or décolletage

during this transition?

0=No change · 4=Significant new

hyperpigmentation covering multiple

areas

Hormonal melasma intensifies in

perimenopause due to erratic

estrogen fluctuations stimulating

melanocytes. Estrogen spikes in early

perimenopause can reach double

normal levels before the eventual fall

— these spikes overstimulate

pigmentation. This symptom is a

visible marker of estrogen

FLUCTUATION (not just deficiency)

— characteristic of early-to-mid

perimenopause, often predating hot

flashes.

71

Skin Luminosity

Loss

Has the natural glow, luminosity,

or radiance of your skin

diminished — appearing flat, dull,

or tired regardless of sleep?

0=No change · 4=Persistently dull skin

that no product can address

Skin luminosity reflects:

microcirculation (reduced by estrogen

loss and iron deficiency), cell turnover

rate (slowed by thyroid decline),

hydration levels (reduced by declining

hyaluronic acid synthesis), and

antioxidant status (reduced by

increased oxidative stress in

menopause). Dull skin unresponsive

to topical products is an internal

systems signal requiring investigation.

72

Sweating Pattern

Change

Apart from hot flashes, have you

noticed changes in how and where

your body sweats — more intense

sweating from specific areas or

decreased sweating elsewhere?

0=No change · 4=Dramatically altered

sweating pattern, very uncomfortable

Eccrine sweat gland function is

modulated by estrogen. As estrogen

declines, gland sensitivity to

temperature stimuli changes

unpredictably — some areas become

hyperactive (scalp particularly), others

underactive. Scalp sweating

specifically can indicate elevated

Joint pain, stiffness, and muscle aches are among the most reported but least

discussed menopause symptoms. Estrogen is anti-inflammatory — its loss unleashes

systemic inflammation in every joint and connective tissue simultaneously. Bone loss

Stiffness

aching joints to loosen up after

waking?

an hour, or stiff most of the morning

Morning stiffness is the classic

signature of estrogen-loss joint

inflammation. Estrogen acts as a

direct anti-inflammatory in synovial

joint tissue — suppressing

prostaglandins and cytokines that

cause stiffness. Its loss allows low-

Joint Pain During

Activity

Do your joints — hands, knees,

hips, shoulders, ankles — ache

during normal daily activities that

previously caused no discomfort?

0=No pain · 4=Constant joint pain,

significantly limits daily activity

Articular cartilage has estrogen

receptors and requires estrogen for

maintenance and lubrication. Without

estrogen, cartilage degrades faster

and synovial fluid thickens, reducing

joint lubrication. This creates the

aching, grinding sensation. Omega-3

deficiency and magnesium deficiency

both independently compound joint

inflammation — addressing both is

faster than waiting for a hormone

panel result.

75

Muscle Aches

Without Exercise

Do you experience widespread

muscle aching, soreness, or

tenderness not related to exercise

— particularly upper arms, thighs,

or back?

0=None · 4=Constant widespread

Widespread myalgia in menopause:

(1) magnesium deficiency —

magnesium is required for muscle

relaxation; (2) vitamin D deficiency —

severe deficiency causes myalgia that

is mistaken for fibromyalgia; (3)

thyroid insufficiency — every muscle

aching, significantly impacts quality of

life

cell needs T3 for energy production,

hypothyroid myalgia is widespread

and constant; (4) statin medications

— deplete CoQ10, causing muscle

pain independently.

76

Decreased Grip

Strength

Has the strength in your hands

noticeably decreased — difficulty

opening jars, turning handles,

gripping during exercise?

0=No change · 4=Significant weakness

making everyday tasks difficult

Grip strength is one of the strongest

longevity predictors in women — more

predictive of 10-year mortality than

many blood markers. Its decline is a

direct sarcopenia marker. Estrogen

and testosterone both maintain hand

muscle strength. Grip strength decline

is a clinical alert for accelerated

physical aging trajectory and is a

strong bone density concern marker

— DEXA scan indicated at score ≥2

over 48.

77

Back Pain (New or

Worsening)

Have you developed new or

significantly worsening lower back

pain — in the spine — not related

to a specific identified injury?

0=No change · 4=Constant severe back

pain, limiting mobility

Vertebral bone density loss begins in

perimenopause and is frequently

asymptomatic until an incident reveals

it. New, non-injury spinal pain in

perimenopause warrants bone density

screening. The lumbar spine is the

first site of estrogen-related bone loss.

Spinal compression can cause height

loss (measured at each Empress

annual review). Back pain + declining

grip strength + age over 48 = DEXA

scan indication in Empress protocol.

78

Frozen Shoulder /

Shoulder Pain

Have you developed shoulder

pain, stiffness, or restricted range

of motion — affecting sleep

position or overhead arm

movement?

0=No change · 4=Severe restriction,

cannot dress without pain, disrupts

sleep

Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)

occurs at 3–5x the rate in

perimenopausal women vs.

premenopausal women. Estrogen

maintains shoulder joint capsule

flexibility and reduces the

inflammatory cytokines that cause

capsular fibrosis. This is an under-

recognised estrogen-deficiency

marker that often goes misdiagnosed

for months. Blood glucose elevation is

also a strong independent risk factor

— HbA1c should be tested.

79

Headaches /

Migraines

Have you developed new

headaches or migraines, or found

existing ones significantly

worsening — particularly at

specific cycle points or when

stress is highest?

0=No change · 4=Frequent debilitating

migraines 2+ per week

Hormonal migraines are driven by the

DROP in estradiol — the withdrawal is

the trigger, not the low level itself. In

perimenopause, estrogen fluctuates

dramatically and unpredictably,

creating multiple potential migraine

triggers per week. Magnesium

deficiency significantly lowers the

migraine threshold — magnesium is

the brain's natural NMDA receptor

antagonist. Magnesium at 600mg/day

has clinical trial evidence for migraine

prophylaxis.

CATEGORY 8 — Genitourinary, Intimate & Sexual Health

10 Markers · #83–92

GSM (Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause) affects up to 50% of menopausal

women and is profoundly underreported. Unlike hot flashes that improve with time,

GSM WORSENS without treatment. Sexual health is a clinical health marker — not a

lifestyle preference — and Empress treats it as such.

80

Plantar Fasciitis /

Foot Pain

Have you developed pain in the

bottom of your feet — particularly

intense on first steps in the

morning — not present before?

0=No · 4=Debilitating morning foot pain,

hobbling on first getting up

Plantar fasciitis incidence spikes in

perimenopause. Estrogen maintains

collagen integrity in the plantar fascia.

Its loss causes the fascia to become

stiffer, less elastic, and more prone to

micro-tears at the heel attachment.

Weight gain (common in menopause)

increases mechanical load on

already-weakened collagen tissue.

This is a collagen + estrogen +

metabolic confluence symptom —

treat all three.

81

Height Loss (Self-

Perceived)

Do you believe you have lost

measurable height — noticeable in

photos, door frames, or self-

measurement?

0=No · 4=Noticeable visible height loss,

greater than expected from aging alone

Height loss is a direct clinical marker

of vertebral bone loss — each

millimetre of height lost represents a

degree of vertebral compression. In

women with significant bone loss, this

precedes fracture. Any self-perceived

height loss in a perimenopausal or

postmenopausal woman is an

immediate DEXA scan indication.

Empress tracks height at onboarding

and annually — this is unique in digital

menopause care.

82

Dental ChangesHave you noticed increased tooth

sensitivity, gum recession,

loosening teeth, or new dental

concerns your dentist has flagged

as unexpected?

0=No change · 4=Significant dental

issues, tooth loss concerns raised

The jaw and alveolar bone (tooth-

supporting bone) are among the most

estrogen-sensitive bones in the body.

Bone loss in the jaw is often the first

bone loss to manifest — dentists can

sometimes identify early osteoporosis

before a DEXA scan. Gum recession

and tooth sensitivity increase with

systemic inflammation and declining

estrogen-supported immune

surveillance in oral tissue. Dental

changes = bone loss proxy.

#Symptom BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Mechanism — Why It Matters

83

Vaginal DrynessHow would you describe vaginal

dryness or discomfort in daily life

— not just during sex?

0=None · 1=Mild occasional ·

2=Noticeable daily · 3=Uncomfortable,

affects activities · 4=Severe — painful

daily, cannot wear certain clothing

The vaginal epithelium is one of the

most estrogen-dependent tissues in

the body. Estrogen maintains wall

(which feeds protective Lactobacillus),

and natural lubrication. Without

estrogen, the tissue atrophies within

months. GSM begins in

perimenopause and PROGRESSES

without intervention — unlike hot

flashes which can improve. This is not

a cosmetic issue — it is a tissue

health emergency.

84

Pain During

Intercourse

Do you experience pain, burning,

or significant discomfort during

sexual intercourse that was not

present or much milder before?

0=No change · 1=Mild · 2=Moderate,

requires adjustment · 3=Significant,

limiting intimacy · 4=Severe — makes

sex impossible

Dyspareunia (painful intercourse) is

the most clinically significant GSM

symptom and the most likely to

permanently alter relationship

intimacy. Vaginal atrophy reduces

tissue resilience and lubrication,

combined with decreased arousal

response from low estrogen and

testosterone. Vaginal estrogen has

near-100% clinical efficacy and

minimal systemic absorption — it is

one of the safest and most effective

interventions in menopause medicine.

85

Libido ChangeHow has your interest in sexual

activity changed — not just

opportunity but genuine internal

spontaneous desire?

0=No change · 1=Slightly reduced ·

2=Noticeably reduced · 3=Rarely

spontaneous · 4=Absent — desire

completely switched off

Female libido is testosterone-driven

— specifically free (bioavailable)

testosterone, not total. Women lose

50% of testosterone by age 40.

Combined with estrogen loss reducing

genital blood flow and physical

arousal sensation, the result can be

complete absence of spontaneous

desire. This is physiological, not

psychological — though relationship

and psychological factors modulate it.

SHBG is critical: high SHBG (common

in perimenopause) binds testosterone,

reducing free levels even when total is

"normal."

86

Arousal DifficultyEven with interest in sex, has

physical arousal — genital

sensation, lubrication, physical

response — significantly

diminished or become much

slower?

0=No change · 4=Very difficult to

achieve physical arousal, greatly

reduced sensation

Physical arousal depends on clitoral

and vaginal blood flow, which is

estrogen-dependent. Estrogen

maintains vascular smooth muscle

function and nitric oxide (NO)

production in genital vasculature.

Without estrogen, blood flow to the

clitoris and vaginal walls reduces

significantly — the physical arousal

response is dampened even when

desire is present. This is the female

equivalent of erectile dysfunction — it

is vascular and hormonal, not

psychological.

87

Urinary UrgencyDo you experience sudden,

urgent, hard-to-control urges to

urinate — even when your bladder

is not full?

0=Never · 1=Occasionally · 2=Several

times/week · 3=Daily, disruptive ·

4=Constantly, controls my entire daily

schedule

The urethra, bladder trigone, and

pelvic floor muscles all have estrogen

receptors. Without estrogen, the

urethral lining thins (atrophic

urethritis), the detrusor muscle

becomes overactive, and pelvic floor

tone declines. This creates urgency-

frequency syndrome — one of the

most quality-of-life-impairing GSM

symptoms. Treating with bladder

medication alone without addressing

the estrogen deficiency produces

inferior results.

88

Recurrent UTIsHave you experienced recurring

UTIs — more than 2 in the past

year — when this was not

previously a pattern?

0=No change · 4=Monthly UTIs,

significantly impacting life

Recurring UTIs in menopause are a

direct consequence of GSM: estrogen

loss depletes vaginal Lactobacillus

(which normally acidify the vaginal

and urethral environment). Without

Lactobacillus, uropathogens like E.

coli colonise easily. Vaginal pH rises

from acidic (3.5–4.5) to alkaline (5.0–

7.0). Vaginal estrogen reduces UTI

recurrence by 50–75% in clinical trials

— it is the definitive treatment, not just

antibiotics.

89

Stress Urinary

Leakage

Do you experience involuntary

urine leakage when coughing,

sneezing, laughing, jumping, or

exercising?

0=Never · 1=Rarely with extreme

exertion · 2=Regularly · 3=With minor

exertion · 4=Even minimal movement

Stress incontinence results from

pelvic floor weakness and reduced

urethral closure pressure — both

maintained by estrogen in periurethral

connective tissue. As estrogen

declines, the pelvic floor undergoes

collagen and elastin loss, reducing its

ability to support the bladder neck

against sudden pressure increases.

This is both preventable (targeted

exercise + estrogen support) and

highly treatable if established.

90

Orgasm ChangesHave you noticed changes in

orgasm — more difficult to

achieve, less intense, or altered

sensation?

0=No change · 4=Unable to achieve

orgasm, no sensation

Orgasm requires pelvic blood flow,

clitoral sensitivity, genital mucosal

health, and neurological

responsiveness — all estrogen and

testosterone dependent. Declining

blood flow to the clitoris reduces

sensitivity; vaginal atrophy reduces

pleasurable sensation; reduced

testosterone lowers the dopaminergic

reward component. This is a

reversible symptom with appropriate

hormonal support — it is not

permanent or inevitable.

91

Pelvic Pressure /

Heaviness

Do you experience a feeling of

pressure, heaviness, or bulging in

the pelvic floor — particularly after

Pelvic organ prolapse risk increases

significantly in menopause as pelvic

floor connective tissue loses estrogen

support. Feeling of pressure or

10 Markers · #93–102

Cardiovascular disease surpasses breast cancer as the #1 killer of women after

menopause — yet it is the least discussed menopause risk. The 10-year cardiovascular

risk profile changes dramatically in the menopausal transition. Every cardiovascular

symptom is a clinical alert that demands investigation, not reassurance.

0=Never · 4=Constant pressure, visible

or palpable prolapse

heaviness after standing indicates

early prolapse or significant pelvic

floor laxity. This is both preventable

with targeted exercise and estrogen

support, and treatable if established.

Score ≥3 = urgent NP assessment

before continuing high-impact

exercise.

92

Vaginal Odour

Change

Have you noticed a change in

vaginal odour — less acidic,

different character — that

concerns you or is new?

0=No change · 4=Significant change,

affecting confidence and daily comfort

Vaginal odour change reflects the shift

in vaginal microbiome from

Lactobacillus-dominant (producing

lactic acid = healthy acidic odour) to

mixed flora (alkaline, different smell).

This pH shift is direct GSM —

estrogen-dependent Lactobacillus

declines without estrogen support. BV

(bacterial vaginosis) rates increase in

menopausal women by 2–3x. This is

not a hygiene issue — it is a hormonal

microbiome event that requires

hormonal and probiotic intervention.

#Symptom BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Mechanism — Why It Matters

93

Heart Palpitations

(At Rest)

Do you experience your heart

racing, fluttering, or pounding at

rest — not triggered by a hot flash

or exertion?

0=Never · 4=Daily concerning episodes

Estrogen modulates cardiac ion

channels — without it, the heart

becomes more susceptible to

arrhythmic disturbances. Magnesium

deficiency (very common in

menopause) lowers the electrical

threshold for palpitations. TSH must

be ruled out urgently — both

hypothyroid and hyperthyroid cause

palpitations. Score ≥2 = TSH + ECG

minimum. Do not attribute to anxiety

before cardiac causes are excluded.

94

Dizziness on

Standing

Do you experience

lightheadedness, dizziness, or

near-fainting when standing up

Orthostatic hypotension (blood

pressure drop on standing) worsens in

menopause — estrogen normally

from sitting or lying down?

0=Never · 4=Every time I stand, must

pause to avoid falling

maintains vascular tone and the

baroreceptor reflex that compensates

for positional changes. Dehydration

(worsened by night sweats) and iron-

deficiency anaemia are major

contributors. Score ≥3 = blood

pressure monitoring (lying + standing)

and NP assessment urgently.

95

Breathlessness at

Usual Exertion

Have you become breathless

during activities that previously

required no conscious respiratory

effort — climbing stairs, moderate

walking, carrying items?

0=No change · 4=Breathless with

minimal exertion

New breathlessness always requires

clinical evaluation. Most common

causes in menopause: iron-deficiency

anaemia (reduced oxygen-carrying

capacity), thyroid disease,

deconditioning, or — importantly —

cardiac or pulmonary changes. Never

assume breathlessness is "just

menopause" without investigation.

Score ≥2 = NP assessment

mandatory before attributing to

hormonal cause alone.

96

Whole-Body

Fatigue (Not

Sleepiness)

Do you experience deep physical

whole-body exhaustion — not the

same as sleepiness — that limits

capacity to function even after

rest?

0=Normal energy · 4=Profound

disabling fatigue present most days

The 5-cause framework for profound

fatigue in menopause: (1) thyroid

insufficiency — every cell needs T3;

(2) iron deficiency — oxygen delivery

to tissues; (3) adrenal insufficiency —

insufficient cortisol for sustained

alertness; (4) sleep quality failure —

insufficient restorative sleep stages;

(5) insulin resistance — cellular

energy production impairment. All five

can coexist. Score ≥3 =

comprehensive fatigue panel, not

reassurance.

97

Blood Pressure

Changes

Have you been told your blood

pressure has increased, or do you

experience headaches, vision

changes, or pressure sensations

in the head?

0=No change / Always normal ·

4=Hypertension confirmed or new

severe symptoms

Cardiovascular risk increases sharply

at menopause — estrogen's

vasodilatory, anti-inflammatory, and

anti-atherosclerotic effects are lost

simultaneously. Blood pressure rises

on average 5–8 mmHg in the first 3

years. Visceral fat increases

inflammatory cytokines that stiffen

arterial walls. Score ≥2 = home blood

pressure monitoring and lipid panel

within this appointment.

98

Exercise Recovery

Time

How long does your body take to

recover from a moderate workout

— muscle soreness, fatigue,

return to baseline?

0=24 hrs as before · 1=36–48 hrs ·

2=48–72 hrs · 3=3–5 days · 4=Over 5

days, excessive soreness

Recovery capacity is a composite

marker of mitochondrial efficiency,

muscle glycogen replenishment, anti-

inflammatory capacity, and hormonal

anabolism (testosterone, estrogen,

IGF-1 all contribute to muscle repair).

In menopause, all decline. Wearable

HRV is the best objective measure —

low morning HRV indicates the body

has not recovered from the previous

day's load and additional exercise will

increase injury risk.

99

Chest Tightness /

Discomfort

Do you experience any chest

tightness, pressure, or discomfort

— even if brief — that is new in

this transition?

0=Never · 4=Regular episodes that

concern me

Any new chest symptoms in a

perimenopausal woman require

cardiac evaluation before attributing to

menopause. Women's cardiac

symptoms are atypical — chest

tightness, jaw pain, and fatigue are

the female presentation of ischaemic

heart disease. Menopause

accelerates atherosclerosis

significantly. The average female

cardiac event occurs 10 years later

than males but is more often fatal

because it is more often missed and

misattributed to anxiety.

100

HRV — Wearable

Baseline

If you have a wearable device,

what is your average morning

HRV reading?

Baseline capture for longitudinal

tracking — absolute value less

important than personal downward

trend over weeks

HRV is the gold-standard non-

invasive marker of autonomic nervous

system health, recovery status, and

cardiovascular resilience. Declining

HRV trend over weeks/months is an

early indicator of increasing allostatic

load — the cumulative physiological

burden of sleep deprivation, cortisol

dysregulation, and metabolic stress.

Empress tracks this as an objective

biomarker alongside subjective

symptom scores — the first digital

menopause platform to do so

systematically.

101

Resting Heart Rate

Trend

Has your resting heart rate

increased noticeably compared to

1–2 years ago — based on

wearable data or awareness?

0=No change · 4=Significantly elevated,

wearable confirms increase

Elevated resting HR in menopause

can reflect: deconditioning (loss of

cardiovascular fitness), iron-deficiency

anaemia (heart compensates with

higher rate), thyroid dysfunction

(hyperthyroid = elevated HR), or

chronic cortisol elevation (sympathetic

dominance). A rising resting HR trend

on a wearable device is one of the

most objective early indicators of

declining cardiovascular health in this

population.

102

Blood Pressure

Variability

Do you experience episodes

where your blood pressure feels

very different — sometimes head

pressure, sometimes feeling faint

— without a consistent pattern?

0=Stable · 4=Highly variable, difficult to

manage daily activities around it

Erratic blood pressure variability in

perimenopause is driven by estrogen

fluctuation affecting vasomotor tone

— on high-estrogen days, vessels

dilate (potentially feeling faint); on low-

estrogen days or with hot flash

events, vessels constrict (head

pressure). This variability is a marker

of perimenopause progression and

often resolves with estrogen

stabilisation via HRT.

CATEGORY 10 — Lifestyle, Gut Health & Nutritional

Context 18 Markers · #103–120

These markers contextualise everything else. The same hormonal environment

produces dramatically different outcomes depending on lifestyle inputs. Alcohol, sleep

hygiene, protein, plant diversity, movement, and stress management are not soft

#Lifestyle / Gut BiomarkerQuestion We AskClinical Signal — Why It Matters

103

Alcohol IntakeHow many units of alcohol do you

consume weekly?

0=None · 1=1–5 units · 2=6–10 units ·

3=11–15 units · 4=Over 15 units

Alcohol is simultaneously: a

direct estrogen metabolism

disruptor (impairs hepatic

clearance), a sleep destroyer

(suppresses REM and deep

sleep), a hot flash amplifier

(triggers vasomotor events), a

gut microbiome disruptor (kills

Lactobacillus), and a liver burden

(elevates GGT). Even 5–10

units/week is clinically

meaningful in estrogen-deficient

women. Score ≥2 = alcohol

reduction protocol before any

other lifestyle intervention.

104

Protein Intake

Adequacy

Do you eat a protein source at

every meal?

0=Every meal 30g+ · 1=Most meals ·

2=Once daily · 3=A few times/week ·

4=Rarely — not intentional about

protein

Protein is the single most critical

macronutrient in menopause —

for muscle preservation,

hormone synthesis,

neurotransmitter synthesis,

satiety (overrides leptin

resistance), and bone matrix.

Women in menopause need 1.2–

1.6g/kg/day minimum, double

the standard RDA. Score ≥2 =

dietary protein intervention is the

highest-priority nutritional action.

Nothing else works well without

adequate protein foundation.

105

Phytoestrogen Food

Intake

How often do you eat

phytoestrogenic foods — flaxseed,

soy, edamame, chickpeas,

pomegranate, sesame?

0=Daily multiple sources ·

1=Several/week · 2=Occasionally ·

3=Rarely · 4=Never / unfamiliar with

these foods

Dietary phytoestrogens bind

estrogen receptors weakly and

can reduce hot flash frequency

by 20–40% in clinical trials.

Ground flaxseed (lignans) is the

most evidence-backed single

food for hot flash reduction. The

key: it must be ground to be

bioavailable. Score ≥3 =

significant untapped, zero-cost

dietary intervention available

immediately at every

supermarket.

106

Gut Plant Food

Diversity

How many different plant foods —

vegetables, fruits, grains,

legumes, nuts, seeds — do you

eat in a typical week?

0=30+ different plants · 1=20–30 ·

2=10–20 · 3=5–10 · 4=Under 5 plant

varieties per week

The estrobolome — gut bacteria

responsible for estrogen

recirculation — determines how

effectively estrogen is

metabolised and reactivated.

Women with higher plant

diversity have better estrogen

metabolism, lower systemic

inflammation, and less severe

menopause symptoms. The

target of 30+ different plants per

week is the research-backed

threshold for maintaining

estrobolome diversity. This single

metric has downstream effects

on every biomarker in this

framework.

107

Fermented Food

Intake

How often do you consume

fermented foods — yogurt, kefir,

kimchi, sauerkraut, miso,

kombucha?

0=Daily · 1=Several/week ·

2=Occasionally · 3=Rarely · 4=Never

Fermented foods directly supply

live bacteria to the gut —

specifically Lactobacillus strains

that support estrobolome

function, produce short-chain

fatty acids, and maintain

intestinal barrier integrity.

Stanford research demonstrated

that fermented food consumption

reduces systemic inflammation

markers (hs-CRP, IL-6)

independently of other dietary

factors within 10 weeks. This is a

modifiable behaviour with rapid

measurable impact.

108

Screen Exposure

Before Bed

How long before bed do you stop

using screens — phone, tablet,

TV, laptop?

Blue light from screens

suppresses melatonin production

by up to 50% for up to 3 hours

after exposure. In estrogen-

deficient women whose

melatonin production is already

reduced by 25–50%, screen use

before bed can shift sleep onset

by 1–2 hours and reduce deep

sleep percentage significantly.

This is a fully modifiable

behaviour with near-immediate

measurable impact on every

sleep metric.

109

Morning Sunlight

Exposure

Do you get outside and see

natural daylight within 30–60

minutes of waking?

0=Every morning · 1=Most mornings ·

2=Several/week · 3=Rarely · 4=Almost

Morning light exposure is the

primary entrainment signal for

the circadian clock — it triggers

the cortisol awakening response

(CAR) and sets the 16-hour

countdown to melatonin release.

Without morning light, the

never — often don't go outside until

afternoon

cortisol rhythm loses its anchor

and becomes dysregulated. This

is a completely free intervention

with clinical evidence for

improving cortisol rhythm, sleep

onset, and mood. Score ≥3 =

biggest free intervention

available.

110

Daily Movement

Pattern

Describe your average daily

movement — sitting, standing, and

deliberate movement throughout

the day.

0=Highly active all day · 1=Active with

some sitting · 2=Mostly sitting with

walks · 3=Mostly sedentary · 4=Almost

entirely sedentary — desk work, no

deliberate daily movement

Prolonged sitting is

independently associated with

worsening insulin resistance,

rising visceral fat, elevated

inflammatory markers, and

declining HRV — regardless of

whether the person exercises for

30 minutes. Movement snacks

(2-min walks every 30 mins)

have clinical evidence for

glucose and inflammatory

marker reduction equal to a

single 30-min walk. In

menopause, sedentary

behaviour compounds every

metabolic risk from Cat 5.

111

Resistance Training

Frequency

How many times per week do you

do resistance or strength training

— weights, bands, bodyweight

exercises against resistance?

0=3+ times/week · 1=2 times/week ·

2=Once/week · 3=Rarely · 4=Never

Resistance training is the single

most evidence-backed lifestyle

intervention in menopause —

arguably equal to HRT for

metabolic outcomes. Benefits:

increases muscle mass

(reverses sarcopenia), improves

insulin sensitivity, reduces

visceral fat, raises bone density,

improves deep sleep by 20–

30%, reduces hot flash

frequency, and improves mood

via BDNF and endorphin

release. Score ≥2 = highest-

priority lifestyle recommendation

in this entire framework.

112

Stress Management

Practice

Do you have a regular daily

practice for managing stress —

breathwork, meditation, yoga,

journaling, or similar?

0=Daily consistent practice ·

1=Several/week · 2=Occasionally when

very stressed · 3=Rarely · 4=Never —

no practice

Chronic unmanaged stress is the

primary cortisol driver — and

elevated cortisol suppresses

progesterone (they share a

biosynthetic precursor,

pregnenolone, and cortisol

"steals" it), worsens insulin

resistance, disrupts sleep,

accelerates bone loss, and

directly worsens vasomotor

symptoms. A daily 5-minute

breathing practice has clinical

evidence for cortisol reduction

and HRV improvement. Score ≥3

= cortisol dysregulation is almost

certainly contributing to the

majority of other symptom

scores in this assessment.

113

Social Connection

Quality

How connected, supported, and

understood do you feel by the

people in your life regarding what

you are going through?

0=Well-supported and understood ·

4=Completely alone — no one

understands, not talking to anyone

about this

Social isolation directly predicts

elevated inflammatory markers

(IL-6, CRP), cortisol

dysregulation, and worsened

sleep quality — independent of

all other factors. Loneliness has

measurable biological effects

equivalent in mortality risk to

smoking 15 cigarettes per day. In

menopause specifically, women

who report feeling supported

have lower symptom severity

scores across every domain.

Peer connection is a clinical

intervention.

114

Hydration AdequacyHow much plain water do you

drink daily?

0=Over 2L · 1=1.5–2L · 2=1–1.5L ·

3=Under 1L · 4=Rarely drink plain water

Dehydration worsens hot flash

severity (dehydrated blood

impairs temperature regulation),

worsens brain fog (brain

performance drops measurably

at 1–2% dehydration), worsens

urinary symptoms (concentrated

urine irritates the bladder), and

worsens skin appearance. Night

sweats create significant fluid

loss — a woman losing

500ml/night in sweat requires

active replacement above

baseline daily fluid intake.

115

Caffeine TimingWhat time is your last caffeinated

drink of the day?

0=Before noon · 1=Before 1pm ·

2=Before 3pm · 3=After 3pm · 4=After

5pm or with dinner

Caffeine has a half-life of 5–7

hours — a 3pm coffee retains

50% of its cortisol-spiking and

adenosine-blocking effect at 8–

9pm. In menopausal women

whose sleep architecture is

already fragile and cortisol

rhythm already dysregulated,

late caffeine is a directly

traceable sleep disruptor that

also worsens evening anxiety

and the wired-but-tired pattern.

Score ≥3 = caffeine curfew is a

top-priority immediate

intervention.

116

Omega-3 Food /

Supplement Intake

How often do you eat fatty fish —

salmon, mackerel, sardines,

herring — or use a high-quality

omega-3 supplement?

0=Daily supplement + 3+ fish

meals/week · 1=Supplement or 2+ fish

meals · 2=1 fish meal/week · 3=Rarely

Omega-3 (EPA + DHA) has

clinical evidence for reducing hot

flash severity, lowering systemic

inflammation (hs-CRP),

improving brain function (DHA is

structural in neuronal

membranes), supporting joint

health, improving mood (EPA

eat fish, no supplement · 4=Never

consume fish or omega-3 supplements

specifically), reducing

cardiovascular risk (ApoB,

triglycerides), and improving skin

barrier function. It is the single

most broadly impactful

supplement in menopausal

women — addressing 6+

symptom categories

simultaneously.

117

Gut Symptoms —

Frequency &

Character

Have you noticed changes in

bowel habits, bloating, IBS-like

symptoms, or gut discomfort that

are new or significantly worse in

this transition?

0=No change · 4=Daily significant gut

symptoms impacting quality of life

The gut is profoundly hormone-

sensitive — estrogen and

progesterone receptors are

throughout the GI tract. Declining

estrogen slows gastric motility,

reduces acid production, and

alters gut microbiome

composition. Declining

progesterone removes its

smooth muscle relaxant effect on

the gut. The result: constipation,

bloating, increased gut

sensitivity, and worsening IBS in

previously controlled patients.

New gut symptoms in

perimenopause are hormonal

until proven otherwise.

118

content:
Do you maintain consistent sleep

and wake times — within 30

minutes — even on weekends?

0=Very consistent — same time 7

days/week · 1=Mostly consistent ·

2=Weekends vary by 1–2 hours ·

3=Weekend sleep pattern very different

· 4=No consistent sleep schedule at all

Circadian rhythm consistency is

the foundation of sleep quality.

"Social jetlag" — the discrepancy

between weekday and weekend

sleep times — has been shown

to independently elevate insulin

resistance, increase cortisol

dysregulation, and worsen sleep

quality even when total sleep

time is adequate. In menopausal

women with already-disrupted

circadian rhythms, weekend

sleep schedule variability

prevents recovery and

perpetuates the dysregulation.

119

Mindful Eating / Meal

Pacing

Do you eat meals slowly and

mindfully — or do you tend to eat

quickly, distracted, or on the go?

0=Always slow, mindful, seated meals ·

4=Almost always eating quickly,

distracted, standing, or skipping meals

Slow eating directly improves

satiety signalling — it takes 15–

20 minutes for the brain to

register fullness from CCK and

leptin signals. Fast eating

bypasses this, leading to

overconsumption. Distracted

eating specifically increases

cortisol and impairs digestive

enzyme secretion (reduced

gastric acid = reduced protein

and mineral absorption). In

women already leptin-resistant

from menopause, slow eating is

a clinical intervention, not a

Purpose & Meaning

Score

How connected do you feel to a

sense of purpose, meaning, or

fulfilment in your daily life —

beyond tasks and responsibilities?

0=Strong, clear sense of purpose ·

4=Have lost my sense of purpose

entirely, feel directionless

Purpose and meaning have

direct, measurable biological

effects — higher purpose scores

are associated with lower IL-6,

lower cortisol reactivity, better

sleep quality, and lower mortality

risk in longitudinal studies. In

menopause, where identity

disconnection (marker #48) is

common, loss of purpose

compounds every hormonal

symptom by elevating baseline

cortisol and reducing the

motivation to implement the

interventions that would help.

This marker closes the loop

between clinical and existential.

Empress addresses both.
